---
title: "Path To UTMB"
---

:::{=html}
<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/font-awesome/6.5.1/css/all.min.css" integrity="sha512-9usAa8m0M+WyW59Ry...cut..." crossorigin="anonymous" referrerpolicy="no-referrer" />
:::

```{=html}
<div class="project-link-container">
    <i class="fab fa-github"></i>
    <span> Check out the full 
    <a href="https://github.com/1312Bravo/Path-To-UTMB" target="_blank"> GitHub repository </a>
    for this project</span>
</div>
```


::: {.blog-card}
[**Scrape UTMB Race and Runners statistics - Data**](path_to_utmb/data.qmd)  
Behind every UTMB analysis lies one major challenge: getting the data. A lot of valuable UTMB data exists publicly, but rarely in a format that is easy to analyze. To make large-scale analysis possible, I built a collection of Python functions that scrape and organize race results, runner statistics, and historical performance data directly from UTMB webpages. The pipeline supports multiple races and runner profiles with a simple, reusable interface — turning scattered web data into analysis-ready datasets.
<span class="blog-date">2026-05-12</span>
:::

::: {.blog-card}
[**What UTMB Index Do I Need to Break 27 Hours With Almost Certainty**](path_to_utmb/sub27_prob.qmd)  
UTMB is a dream race for most of trail runners. Also for me. But I don't want to approach it with the mindset of simply finishing. My goal is to arrive at the start line ready to truly race. I set myself an (perhaps too) ambitious benchmark: I would only commit to UTMB once I believed I could run under 27 hours with near certainty.That naturally led to a more practical question: As UTMB Index is the simplest measurable signal of performance, what value would actually tell I'm ready to realistically expect a sub-27 finish?
<span class="blog-date">2026-05-23</span>
:::
